answer:
The following works in the corpus specifically mention Greek or Roman antiquity (e.g., via phrases like "ancient Greece," "ancient Rome," "ancient Greeks," or "ancient Romans," or direct contextual references to their ancient periods, figures, and societies such as Athens, Sparta, Plato, Aristotle, or Cicero). These primarily occur in discussions of slavery, civilization, and societal progress. No other works contain such references.[^1][^2][^3][^4][^5]

- **Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation*, Vol. III** (Ch. 48 references "ancient Greece").[^6]
- **Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation*, Vol. IV** (Ch. 15 and 23 reference "ancient Rome" and "ancient Romans"; Ch. 23 also references Sparta and Cicero).[^7][^8]
- **Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie sociale*, Vol. I** (Ch. 7 references "ancient Greeks").[^9]
- **Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie sociale*, Vol. II** (Ch. 18 references "ancient Greeks").[^10]
- **Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, Vol. I** (Ch. 1 references Plato, Aristotle, and Cicero as slaveholders of antiquity).[^11]
- **Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation*, Vol. I** (multiple chapters reference Plato, Cicero, and Sparta as exemplars of antiquity).[^12]
- **Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation*, Vol. II** (Ch. 1 references Sparta and Athens).[^13]

[^1]: No hits for "Greek antiquity," "Roman antiquity," "ancient Greek," or "ancient Roman." Searches for "ancient Greece," "ancient Rome," "ancient Greeks," and "ancient Romans" yielded the results cited here; a fallback search for "Athens OR Sparta OR Plato OR Aristotle OR Cicero" surfaced additional contextual matches in the same works.
[^2]: The corpus's 37 works focus on post-classical philosophy (mostly 18th-19th century French liberal thought), so classical antiquity is referenced illustratively rather than as primary subject matter.
[^3]: Full list of works available via `list_works`; no other slugs matched.
[^4]: Matches are from English translations only (`scope: "english"`).
[^5]: Excludes non-historical mentions (e.g., poetic reference to Plato in *Diwan-E-Zauq*).
[^6]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL III*, [p-a92f3f](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iii-ca589a/48-de-la-superiorite-des-peuples-d-espece-caucasienne/translation/#p-a92f3f).
[^7]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL IV*, [p-15a1d3](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iv-bca040/15-de-l-influence-de-l-esclavage-domestique-sur-l-esp/translation/#p-15a1d3).
[^8]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL IV*, [p-bed00d](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iv-bca040/23-de-l-inegalite-des-fortunes-produite-par-l-esclava/translation/#p-bed00d) and [p-2bb84e](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iv-bca040/23-de-l-inegalite-des-fortunes-produite-par-l-esclava/translation/#p-2bb84e).
[^9]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [p-6c6409](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/07-ii-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des/translation/#p-6c6409).
[^10]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, [p-483a76](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/18-viii-application-des-memes-moyens-de-liberte-aux-a/translation/#p-483a76).
[^11]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [p-fcbad4](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/01-chapitre-premier-de-linfluence-des-doctrines-des-p/translation/#p-fcbad4).
[^12]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, e.g., [p-74b833](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/09-de-la-doctrine-qui-fonde-la-morale-et-la-legislati/translation/#p-74b833) and [p-847990](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/12-de-la-description-des-lois-des-effets-qu-elles-pro/translation/#p-847990).
[^13]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL II*, [p-b07df5](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/01-de-ce-qui-constitue-le-perfectionnement-et-la-degr/translation/#p-b07df5).

args.query: Athens OR Sparta OR "ancient Greek" OR "ancient Roman" OR Plato OR Aristotle OR Cicero
